thread.title: Wabanaki Alliance Exec. Director testifies at Congressional hearing: "It felt very good to be seen and heard." | Local News | foxbangor.com
thread.title_full: Wabanaki Alliance Exec. Director testifies at Congressional hearing: "It felt very good to be seen and heard." | Local News | foxbangor.com

author: Cali Warren
title: Wabanaki Alliance Exec. Director testifies at Congressional hearing: "It felt very good to be seen and heard." | Local News | foxbangor.com
text:
INDIAN ISLAND, PENOBSCOT NATION -- November is National Native American Heritage Month, and Maulian Bryant, Executive Director of the Wabanaki Alliance, is shining a spotlight on violence against indigenous women and people. Bryant testified on this issue this week at a congressional hearing.
"It felt very good to be seen and heard as somebody from Maine, as a Wabanaki woman, as somebody from the East," shared Bryant.
She was the only attendee from the Northeast at the Subcommittee on Interior, Environment, and Related Agencies of the US House Committee on Appropriations, US House of Representatives hearing on November 20.
"When an Indigenous woman goes missing there is not the same attention and action as when a Caucasian woman is. The primary reasons for this are three-fold: societal indifference, jurisdictional and coordination issues, and a lack of resources for tribal law enforcement agencies," she stated in her testimony.
And from Washington D.C.to Maine's Penobscot Nation, she's bringing attention to the violence Native women face in the U.S.
"I have three daughters, they're two, 15, and 18...so the statistic is one in three Indigenous women will be the victim of a crime, and I'm always hoping it's me. That I'm saving them from that. Because there's 4 in my family," Bryant stated.
Indigenous women are much more likely to experience violence in their lifetimes. Statistics from the U.S. Department of the Interior show that 84% of Indigenous women have faced some form of violence in their lifetime. 56% have faced sexual violence, and the second leading cause of death for both Native women and men is homicide.
"Sometimes when people hear about these horrible things there can be guilt or shame or not knowing how to deal with these feelings, and all we can do is understand the truths about everyone around us and then go forward together," Bryant shared. She encourages folks to educate themselves on history and contemporary Native happenings via the Wabanaki Alliance website.
